Screenshot of test failing with 100% coverage report

3 min read
7 hours ago

--

Imagine this: You’ve just finished a sleek new component using the latest Angular Signals. You run your tests, and your coverage report glows with a beautiful, solid 100% green. You’re a testing legend!
Then you look at your test runner.
FAILED: should create.
Wait, what? How can code be 100% “covered” if the component can’t even be created? Welcome to the “False Green” trap I recently ran into — a unique quirk of modern Angular testing.
The Culprit: input.required
In modern Angular, we’ve moved away from the optional nature of @Input() to the strict world of Signal Inputs. Specifically, input.required(). I recently created a socials component in my app — in short; I loop through each ‘social’ item in an array and show a specific icon as a link to a particular social page. The data is passed directly to the SocialsComponent from the parent:
export class SocialsComponent {
// A strict contract: this component CANNOT exist without data
socials = input.required<Social[]>();
}This is a powerful feature. It creates a contract: this component is “dumb” and relies entirely on its parent to “feed” it. No data, no component.
The Explosion: Why Coverage Lies
When generating the component, Angular gave me an “out-of-the-box” spec file. When I run that test against a required input, the JavaScript engine (Istanbul) starts tracking.

• The Constructor Runs: The engine sees the class being initialized. Line covered!

• The Signal Setup Runs: The engine sees the input being declared. Line covered!

• The Crash: Angular realizes there is no data for that .required() input and throws a fatal error.
The coverage tool is literal. It asks: “Did the engine execute this line?” The answer is yes, even if that execution ended in a total meltdown a millisecond later. It’s like a car that explodes the moment you turn the ke. Technically, the engine “ran” for a split second before blowing up — so we get a big tick for that.
The “Required” Crash
In the default beforeEach block, we typically see this:
beforeEach(async () => {
fixture = TestBed.createComponent(SocialsComponent);
component = fixture.componentInstance;
fixture.detectChanges(); // <-- THE POINT OF FAILURE
});The moment fixture.detectChanges() is called, Angular attempts to initialise the component lifecycle. It looks for the socials data, finds undefined, and realises it’s violating the contract and our ‘car engine’ blows up.
The Fix: Satisfy the Hunger
To fix this, we must change our testing rhythm. We need to act as the “Parent” and feed the component before it opens its eyes.
1. The “Lazy Initialization” Strategy
First, remove fixture.detectChanges() from your beforeEach. We don’t want the lifecycle to start until we are ready.
2. Manual Feeding
In the it block, use the modern setInput API to provide the data.
it('should create', () => {
// 1. Satisfy the "required" hunger FIRST
fixture.componentRef.setInput('socials', []); // 2. NOW tell the component to open its eyes
fixture.detectChanges();
expect(component).toBeTruthy();
});
The Signal Lifecycle Visualization
If you’re struggling to remember the order, use this mental map for Signal Inputs. Since they are strict, the order is non-negotiable:

• TestBed.createComponent: The component is Born (constructor runs).

• setInput(): The Hunger is satisfied.

• fixture.detectChanges(): The Eyes Open (HTML is rendered).

• expect(): The test checks what the eyes see.
Summary: Coverage ≠ Correctness
100% coverage is a great metric, but it’s not a guarantee of a working app. When working with Signal Inputs:

• Don’t detect changes too early.

• Always feed your required inputs manually.

• Trust your test runner over your coverage report.
By understanding the “False Green” trap, you can write more resilient tests and take full advantage of the type-safety and strictness that Angular Signals provide.
